messages.content: Jolanta Wadowska-Król, a doctor from Katowice, received the title of Doctor Honoris Causa of the University of Silesia on June 24, 2021. This most important honor in the Polish academic world was awarded to her for the fight for the health of the children of Szopienice and Burowiec, started in the mid-1970s. She identified the lead they had and took action to improve their health. In fact, she almost alone faced the party-economic bureaucracy, which did not want to reveal that under the socialist system, children could be affected by the disease caused by industrial pollution. Supported by her closest associates and by Professor Bożena Hager-Małecka, who supervised her research (an outstanding pediatrician and at the same time an influential member of the Sejm), she led to the resettlement of the most vulnerable residents (several buildings were demolished) to new apartments in a healthier environment and to the start of the process of saving children (treatment, trips to preventoria in the mountains).  - rephrease this

Jolanta Wadowska-Król, a doctor hailing from Katowice, was awarded the prestigious title of Doctor Honoris Causa by the University of Silesia on June 24, 2021. This esteemed recognition in the Polish academic realm was granted in recognition of her dedicated efforts towards improving the health of children residing in Szopienice and Burowiec, which she initiated in the 1970s. Upon discovering the presence of lead poisoning among the children due to industrial pollution, she fearlessly confronted the barriers posed by party-economic bureaucracy, who were reluctant to acknowledge such consequences within the socialist system. With the support of her loyal team and Professor Bożena Hager-Małecka, who supervised her research and held influential positions in both pediatric medicine and the Sejm, Dr. Wadowska-Król successfully facilitated the relocation of the most vulnerable residents to healthier environments by demolishing several buildings. Additionally, she spearheaded efforts to provide necessary medical treatments and organized trips to preventoria in the mountains for the affected children, effectively initiating their path to recovery.

Jolanta Wadowska-Król, a doctor from Katowice, received the title of Doctor Honoris Causa of the University of Silesia on June 24, 2021. This most important honor in the Polish academic world was awarded to her for the fight for the health of the children of Szopienice and Burowiec, started in the mid-1970s. She identified the lead they had and took action to improve their health. In fact, she almost alone faced the party-economic bureaucracy, which did not want to reveal that under the socialist system, children could be affected by the disease caused by industrial pollution. Supported by her closest associates and by Professor Bożena Hager-Małecka, who supervised her research (an outstanding pediatrician and at the same time an influential member of the Sejm), she led to the resettlement of the most vulnerable residents (several buildings were demolished) to new apartments in a healthier environment and to the start of the process of saving children (treatment, trips to preventoria in the mountains).  - rephrase this

On June 24, 2021, Jolanta Wadowska-Król, a doctor from Katowice, was bestowed with the esteemed title of Doctor Honoris Causa by the University of Silesia. This remarkable accolade represents the highest honor within the Polish academic realm and was granted to Dr. Wadowska-Król in recognition of her tireless advocacy for the health and well-being of the children residing in Szopienice and Burowiec, a cause she began championing in the 1970s. Recognizing the presence of lead contamination and its adverse effects on the children's health, Dr. Wadowska-Król fearlessly confronted the obstructive party-economic bureaucracy that sought to conceal the detrimental impact of industrial pollution on children under the socialist system. With the unwavering support of her trusted colleagues and the guidance of Professor Bożena Hager-Małecka, an esteemed pediatrician and influential member of the Sejm, Dr. Wadowska-Król successfully orchestrated the relocation of the most vulnerable residents to new, healthier living spaces by demolishing several buildings. Furthermore, she initiated vital measures to ensure the well-being of the affected children through medical treatment and organized trips to preventoria in the mountains, marking the commencement of their road to recovery.

She paid a high price for it. She was prevented from defending her completed doctoral thesis on lead among children. The results of her many years of research have been lost somewhere in the archives of the medical school.  Until the turn of 1989, they were not allowed to be mentioned. The case has come back only in recent years. First, in 2020, Michał Jędryka's book Ołowiane Dzieci (2020) was published, which is an interesting study of the discovery of one's own identity by a lead woman experienced in childhood.  Then, along with the university initiative, there were reports, memoirs and other (but less successful) novels that discounted the popularity of the topic (Fox 2021; Majcher 2023).  - rephrase this

However, she endured significant repercussions for her actions. Dr. Wadowska-Król was barred from defending her completed doctoral thesis on lead poisoning among children, and the invaluable findings from her years of research were lost within the archives of the medical school. It was forbidden to even mention these results until the political shift in 1989. The case only resurfaced in recent years, with the publication of Michał Jędryka's book, "Ołowiane Dzieci" (2020), which delves into the struggle of a lead-affected individual in discovering their own identity. Additionally, the university initiative sparked the emergence of reports, memoirs, and other novels (though less successful) that attempted to shed light on the topic (Fox 2021; Majcher 2023), albeit with less resonance.

Honoring a retired pediatrician by the University of Silesia,  inspired a group of scientists to launch an interdisciplinary research project: Impact of long-term emission of pollutants - environmental, health and socio-cultural effects of non-ferrous metallurgy , in which a multi-faceted analysis of the effects of the impact of the non-ferrous metallurgical plant in Szopienice was made. Sociologists were also invited to participate in the project, and they were commissioned to analyze the collective memory of the inhabitants of the metallurgical settlement   - rephrase this

The recognition of a retired pediatrician by the University of Silesia served as inspiration for a team of scientists to initiate a collaborative and interdisciplinary research project titled "Impact of Long-Term Emission of Pollutants: Environmental, Health, and Socio-Cultural Effects of Non-Ferrous Metallurgy." The project involved conducting a comprehensive analysis of the effects caused by the non-ferrous metallurgical plant in Szopienice. The research team recognized the importance of including sociologists in the project, who were tasked with examining the collective memory of the settlement's residents in relation to the effects of the metallurgical operations.

. Despite this, a wealth of material was collected, from which only a small part concerning the memory of the residents about the pollution of the natural environment of Szopienice was selected for analysis. I was especially interested in the possibility of verifying Peter H. Kahn, Jr.'s thesis on occurrence an environmental generational amnesia . In a recent paper published with his PhD student, Kahn wrote: With each ensuing generation, the amount of environmental degradation increases, but each generation tends to perceive that degraded condition as the nondegraded condition, as the normal experience ," (Kahn, Weiss 2017)  - rephrase this

Despite the abundance of collected material, only a fraction of it concerning the recollections of the residents regarding the pollution of Szopienice's natural environment was chosen for analysis. Of particular interest was the opportunity to examine the validity of Peter H. Kahn, Jr.'s thesis regarding the presence of environmental generational amnesia. In a recent publication co-authored with his PhD student, Kahn stated: "With each successive generation, the level of environmental degradation intensifies, yet each generation tends to perceive this degraded state as the undamaged condition, as the normative experience" (Kahn, Weiss 2017).

The theoretical context of the research is determined by the relations between the notions of collective memory, collective forgetting and places of memory.  In the case of the first one, I refer to the tradition started by Maurice Halbwachs. In the preface to his most important work , Social Frames of Memory, he wrote: "it is in society that a person normally acquires memories, recognizes and locates them" (2008: 4). The individual remembers, but being a social being, he remembers within the framework imposed by society. The framework determines the selection of elements from the rich resources of memory and how we interpret its content (Erll 2018: 35).  - rephrase this

The research is situated within the theoretical framework that explores the interrelations between collective memory, collective forgetting, and places of memory. In the case of collective memory, the groundwork laid by Maurice Halbwachs serves as a reference point. In the preface to his seminal work, "Social Frames of Memory," Halbwachs remarked that "it is within society that an individual typically acquires memories, recognizes them, and situates them" (2008: 4). While memories are experienced on an individual level, they are shaped and influenced by the social context in which one exists. This social framework plays a significant role in determining the selection of specific memories from the vast pool of available resources and influences how we interpret their content (Erll 2018: 35).

Halbwachs expressed it explicitly: in this sense, there would be a collective memory and a social framework of memory, and only to the extent that our individual thought places itself within this framework and participates in this memory, will it be able to remember (35). supra-individual being. Both forms of memory are interdependent (Erll 2018: 35). Individuals remember from the point of view of the group, but at the same time the memory of the group is realized and manifested in individual memories (Halbwachs 2008: 8) - rephrase this

Halbwachs explicitly articulated the concept of both collective memory and the social framework of memory. He highlighted that individuals engage in remembering to the extent that their individual thoughts align with and participate in the larger social framework and collective memory (Erll 2018: 35). There exists a mutually dependent relationship between these two forms of memory (Erll 2018: 35). While individuals remember from the perspective of the group, it is also true that the memory of the group is conveyed and expressed through individual recollections (Halbwachs 2008: 8). The interplay between individual and collective memory is intricately intertwined, with both influences shaping and informing the remembrance process.

Czym w takim razie jest pamięć zbiorowa? Wedle Jacka Nowaka „pamięć zbiorowa jest /…/zestawem wyobrażeń członków wspólnoty o jej przeszłości, uzgadnianym w aktach komunikacji wewnętrznej, przekazywanym w drodze międzypokoleniowej transmisji w celu utrzymania spójnej narracji tożsamościowej” (Nowak 2011: 13) Tożsamość jest przecież konceptualizowana przez wykorzystanie pojęcia trwania w czasie podmiotu (Nowak 2011: 53-54; por. Łuczewski 2017: 128-133). - rephrase this

So what, then, is collective memory? According to Jacek Nowak, "collective memory is a set of imaginings about the past of a community, negotiated through acts of internal communication, transmitted across generations to maintain a coherent narrative of identity" (Nowak 2011: 13). Identity, on the other hand, is conceptualized by utilizing the notion of the subject's continuity over time (Nowak 2011: 53-54; cf. Łuczewski 2017: 128-133).

What then is collective memory? According to Jacek Nowak, "collective memory is /.../a set of ideas of community members about its past, agreed in acts of internal communication, transmitted through intergenerational transmission in order to maintain a coherent identity narrative" (Nowak 2011: 13). After all, identity is conceptualized by using the concept of duration in the time of the subject (Nowak 2011: 53-54; cf. Łuczewski 2017: 128-133). According to Richard Ned Lebow, quoted by Sławomir Kapralski, “understanding the past not only allows us to interpret the present, but also tells us who we are. Shared experiences and memories, values and commitments (…) provide individuals and groups with distinctive identities” (Kapralski: 2012: 54). Participation in memory also confirms belonging to a group.   - rephrase this

So, what exactly is collective memory? Jacek Nowak defines it as "a collection of the ideas held by members of a community about its past, negotiated through internal communication and transmitted across generations in order to maintain a coherent narrative of identity" (Nowak 2011: 13). Implicit in this definition is the understanding that identity is shaped by our perception of continuity over time (Nowak 2011: 53-54; cf. Łuczewski 2017: 128-133). Sławomir Kapralski, drawing on the insights of Richard Ned Lebow, highlights that comprehending the past not only allows us to interpret the present but also plays a crucial role in shaping our individual and group identities. Shared experiences, memories, values, and commitments all contribute to the formation of distinct identities (Kapralski 2012: 54). Active participation in collective memory also serves to reinforce a sense of belonging to a particular group.

When formulating a model of the dynamics of social memory experienced by an individual in a specific culture, Christopher J. Hewer and Ron Roberts drew attention to the various sources of memory. (2012: 171-173). According to the authors, the individual's awareness (subjective reality) in relation to the past is shaped by individual experiences, collective memory functioning in a given community and history (collected in scientific and academic studies). These three spheres influence each other through socially shared knowledge of historical publications and biographical texts, but also through the processes of communication and information exchange between individuals (Hewer, Roberts 2012: 172-173). Thus, individual memory consists of interpenetrating own and mediated experiences (cf. Giddens 2006: 314). - rephrase this

When developing a model for understanding the dynamics of social memory within a specific culture, Christopher J. Hewer and Ron Roberts emphasize the multiple sources that contribute to an individual's perception of the past (2012: 171-173). According to the authors, an individual's awareness, or subjective reality, regarding the past is shaped by their personal experiences, the collective memory of their community, and historical knowledge derived from scientific and academic studies. These three spheres of influence interact with one another through the socially shared knowledge found in historical publications and biographical texts, as well as through the processes of communication and information exchange between individuals (Hewer, Roberts 2012: 172-173). As a result, an individual's memory is comprised of the interconnected combination of their own personal experiences and the mediated experiences they are exposed to (cf. Giddens 2006: 314).

Not only remembering but also forgetting has a social character.  It would be unbearable to remember everything.  Therefore, memory is selective and the "law of necessary forgetting" operates.  From the group's past, only some of the events remain in the social consciousness. The others are repressed somewhere deep in the collective subconscious or completely forgotten.    It is only when collective memory is confronted with historical memory that it is possible to notice the content once repressed or forgotten. Then you can ask questions about the meaning and function of forgetting, reflect on the nature of forgetting. As did Franklin R. Ankersmit (2003) or Paul Connerton (2008).  - rephrase this

Forgetting, like remembering, also possesses a societal dimension. It would be overwhelming to retain every single memory, which is why memory is inherently selective, and the "law of necessary forgetting" comes into play. Within the group's collective past, only certain events endure in the social consciousness, while others may become repressed within the collective subconscious or entirely forgotten. However, when collective memory is confronted with historical memory, previously suppressed or forgotten content can resurface. This then opens up avenues for exploring the significance and function of forgetting and prompts reflections on its nature. Scholars such as Franklin R. Ankersmit (2003) and Paul Connerton (2008) have delved into these inquiries regarding the interplay of memory and forgetting.

From 1834, the center of the Szopienice world was the non-ferrous metal works. It was then that Bergwerksgesellschaft Georg von Giesches Erben launched the Wilhelmina zinc smelter, which was later joined by the Uthemann smelter (cf. Boroń, Grudniewski 2023). The largest complex producing non-ferrous metals in Silesia and the largest producer of cadmium in the world was created. Until 2008, when the non-ferrous metals smelter was put into liquidation, the life of Szopienice revolved around the smelter. The steelworks provided jobs, provided housing, organized free time, children's summer camps. Although there were other workplaces in Szopienice (e.g. Śląskie Zakłady Przemysłu Tłuszczowego (formerly Strahl), vinegar factories, mines), it was the metallurgical conglomerate that determined the nature of the social relations of the village. - rephrase this

Beginning in 1834, the focal point of Szopienice revolved around the non-ferrous metal works. The establishment of the Wilhelmina zinc smelter by Bergwerksgesellschaft Georg von Giesches Erben marked the inception of this industrial presence, later accompanied by the Uthemann smelter (cf. Boroń, Grudniewski 2023). As a result, the largest complex for non-ferrous metal production in Silesia and the world's largest cadmium producer came into being. The smelter remained the lifeblood of Szopienice until its liquidation in 2008.
